coredata.dc:title: Vinyl-functionalized polyphenolic-carbon dot-based fluorometric turn-off–on biosensor for the dual detection of mercury and cysteine and their in vivo sensing in zebrafish larvae
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Anantha Lakshmi, Buddolla
Sangubotla, Roopkumar
Kim, Jongsung
Kim, Young-Joon
coredata.dc:description: The fluorometric turn-off–on biosensor was developed for the ultra-sensitive detection of mercury (Hg2+) and cysteine (Cys) utilizing the highly fluorescent carbon dots (CDs). Herein, the sophisticated low-temperature reflux-mediated reaction was adopted using precursors namely citric acid (CA) and polyphenolic kaempferol (KMP) by using dimethylformamide (DMF) as a solvent. The resulting CDs (i.e., CKCDs) were in the highly negative charged groups (–OH) presented with a bright-orange fluorescence. These CKCDs were functionalized with 4-vinylaniline (4-VA) by employing EDC/NHS coupling reaction, which switched its photoluminescence (PL) towards the strong-blue colored emission and termed as V-CKCDs. The functionalized V-CKCDs can be capable enough to detect mercury via the strong electrostatic interactions between positively charged Hg2+ cations and negatively charged anions (–OH groups). Hence, an adequate fluorescence quenching was observed in V-CKCDs with the lowest concentrations of Hg2+ around 0.5 μM. Significantly, after adding the complex of V-CKCDs-Hg2+ to the Cys, the fluorescence enhancement was observed. This might be attributed from the strong interactions between Hg2+ in the fluorescence sensing system and thiol (-SH) moieties from the Cys. The developed V-CKCDs are highly sensitive for detecting Hg2+ and Cys, which showed detection limits of 10.6 and 42. 48 nM, respectively. Also, the in vivo studies were investigated in zebrafish larvae using V-CKCDs for the detection of Hg2+ and Cys. The V-CKCDs were investigated in the real water samples and human serum to detect Hg2+ and Cys, respectively.

The concentrations were including 0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 3.5 and 4 μM, and (D) linear regression plot between (F-F0)/F0 and Cys concentrations (0–3.5 μM) (0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, and 3.5 μM).

head.ce:title: Vinyl-functionalized polyphenolic-carbon dot-based fluorometric turn-off–on biosensor for the dual detection of mercury and cysteine and their in vivo sensing in zebrafish larvae

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The fluorometric turn-off–on biosensor was developed for the ultra-sensitive detection of mercury (Hg2+) and cysteine (Cys) utilizing the highly fluorescent carbon dots (CDs). Herein, the sophisticated low-temperature reflux-mediated reaction was adopted using precursors namely citric acid (CA) and polyphenolic kaempferol (KMP) by using dimethylformamide (DMF) as a solvent. The resulting CDs (i.e., CKCDs) were in the highly negative charged groups (–OH) presented with a bright-orange fluorescence. These CKCDs were functionalized with 4-vinylaniline (4-VA) by employing EDC/NHS coupling reaction, which switched its photoluminescence (PL) towards the strong-blue colored emission and termed as V-CKCDs. The functionalized V-CKCDs can be capable enough to detect mercury via the strong electrostatic interactions between positively charged Hg2+ cations and negatively charged anions (–OH groups). Hence, an adequate fluorescence quenching was observed in V-CKCDs with the lowest concentrations of Hg2+ around 0.5 μM. Significantly, after adding the complex of V-CKCDs-Hg2+ to the Cys, the fluorescence enhancement was observed. This might be attributed from the strong interactions between Hg2+ in the fluorescence sensing system and thiol (-SH) moieties from the Cys. The developed V-CKCDs are highly sensitive for detecting Hg2+ and Cys, which showed detection limits of 10.6 and 42. 48 nM, respectively. Also, the in vivo studies were investigated in zebrafish larvae using V-CKCDs for the detection of Hg2+ and Cys. The V-CKCDs were investigated in the real water samples and human serum to detect Hg2+ and Cys, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: For many years, an increasing accumulation of hazardous chemicals has been disturbing the global world by affecting the surrounding eco-adversity [1]. In this case, pollution with mercury (Hg2+) ions are responsible for serious health-related dysfunctions in humans including brain, kidney, liver, and other associated major organs [2]. The outstanding chemical stability of these ions in the inorganic form is making them harder to biodegrade posing potentially toxic to humans [3]. Unfortunately, the metallic form of mercury (e.g., methyl mercury) has been largely accumulating in the humans through the consumption of aquatic animals starting from small fish to large mammal species [4]. For instance, its poisoning is responsible for the Minamata disease, a neurological illness found in Japan [5]. Thus, the efficient detection of Hg2+ with high sensitivity is emerging in the field of biomedical and analytical chemistry. So far, atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS), inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy (ICP-MS), calorimetry, chemiluminescence, surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS), and electrochemical techniques are some conventional methods for detecting Hg2+ ions [6–11].

On the other hand, the regulation of redox reactions is greatly promising in the keep up of homeostasis in human. In this regard, cysteine (Cys), a potential thiol-containing amino acid involved in the biological processes and its abnormal concentrations are intricated in the pathophysiological malfunctions like cardiovascular and neurological diseases [12]. Hence, its early and rapid determination is advantageous in dealing with critical neurological disorders. A few existing methods for their detection include electrochemical, SERS, and calorimetry [13–15]. Unfortunately, these methods require excessive sample pre-treatments, complex laborious proceedings with expensive sensing approaches. To address these limitations, fluorescent nanomaterials with superior biocompatibility, extreme sensitivity, cost-effectiveness, and potential physicochemical (i.e., ultra-size and desired surface-modification) and optical properties (e.g., switchable emission wavelength) are urgently considerable.

Previously different nanomaterials based on PL and colorimetry principles, and fluorescent chemosensors were also developed for dual detection of Hg2+ and Cys. For instance, Z. Suo et al. synthesized red fluorescent gold nanoclusters (Au NCs) from a plasma protein i.e., fibrinogen (FBG) as a stabilizer through stirring method. These Au NCs have followed the static quenching with a fluorescence turn-off response against both Hg2+ and Cys with detection limits of 0.15 and 0.79 μM, respectively [16]. Furthermore, the benzimidazole-based chemosensor engaging a chemical reaction between benzene-1,2-diamine and 6-bromopyridine-2-carboxaldehyde was designed for turn-off–on detection of Hg2+ and Cys, which showed the detection limits around 0.74 and 5.2 μM [17]. The other chemosensor was developed using chemical interaction between thiosemicarbazide and 8-hydroxyjulolidine-9-carboxaldehyde for obtaining the on–off mode of sensing against Hg2+ and Cys, which delivered the detection limits of 0.59 and 1.17 μM [18]. Wang and his coworkers designed the colorimetric sensor based on the bovine serum albumin (BSA)-protected Au NCs. The resulting BSA-Au NCs utilizing the catalytic oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the presence of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) for Hg2+ and Cys sensing have shown the detection limits of 30 and 80 nM [19].

Carbon dots (CDs) are the desirable nanomaterials with high photoluminescence (PL)-tunability, shape-specificity, and thermal stability features [20–22]. The CDs are reasonably qualifiable to mention that extremely advantageous as compared to the semiconductor-based quantum dots (QDs), which lack both non-toxicity and biocompatibility [23]. The CDs have already been gaining much attention in the biomedical applications such as wound healing [24,25], drug-delivery [26,27], theranostics [28], biosensing [29], and bio-imaging [30]. For example, Huang et al developed nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) from precursors citric acid, tartaric acid, and ethanediamine through solvothermal method for the “turn-off–on” detection of Hg2+ ions, Cys, and iodide (I-) ions [31]. Li and his coworkers synthesized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from citric acid using pyrolysis method at 200 °C for the detection of Hg2+ and Cys [5]. Zhang et al. developed N-CDs utilizing citric acid and ammonia via hydrothermal method and applied for detecting Hg2+ and Cys [32]. Unfortunately, the use of highly toxic ammonia in their synthesis may have limited their use in studying in vitro or in vivo modalities. T. Liu et al synthesized polymer-like CDs using citric acid as the main carbon-source, magnesium hydroxide [Mg(OH2)] as the chelating molecule, and ethylenediamine as the nitrogenous passivation agent through hydrothermal treatment. As-obtained CDs were employed to detect Hg2+ and Cys based-on the “fluorescence off–on” approach [33].

Recently, the utilization of polyphenolic compounds (e.g., kaempferol, quercetin, and curcumin) in the preparation of diversified nanomaterials is emerging due to their multiple hydroxyl (–OH) groups. They can deliver hydrophilicity and thereby induce the aqueous dispersibility in various kinds of nanomaterials. They can act as stabilizing and reducing agents during the synthesis of nanomaterials. For instance, the CDs, ruthenium-quercetin-based nanoclusters (NCs), and silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) were successfully developed [34–36].

Functionalized CDs have greater scope for detecting specific analytes especially metal ions and thiol-containing compounds. Recently, there has been a raising interest towards the modification of CDs with different organic and aromatic structures for developing analyte-selective sensors [37]. Wang’s research group have developed polyaniline (PANI)/CDs-based nanocomposites for Hg2+ sensing through the convenient interaction between amine moieties of PANI and Hg2+ [38]. In another study, Marieeswaran et al synthesized PANI-nanoclips grafted fluorophore attached single stranded nucleic acid (FAM-ssDNA) for the detection of Hg2+ [39]. However, to the best of our knowledge, the vinyl-functionalized CDs have not been reported for the detection of Hg2+.

In this work, we developed orange-fluorescent CDs (i.e., CKCDs) from citric acid and kaempferol using the DMF as a solvent via reflux reaction. These CKCDs were modified by 4-vinylaniline, which allowed to PL tunability towards the strong blue-color emission. The resulting CDs (i.e., V-CKCDs) were employed as the sensitive “turn-off–on” fluorescent probe for Hg2+ and Cys in aqueous solutions. The feasibility of V-CKCDs for sensing Hg2+ was investigated in real water samples from river and tap water. Also, the detection of Cys was studied in human serum samples to understand their practicality in the biological matrix.

The citric acid (0.256 g) and kaempferol (0.125 g) were mixed in a round bottom flask containing a 40 mL of DMF and refluxed at 50 °C for 24 h. After the reflux, the wine-red colored solution was observed, which indicates the formation of CKCDs. These CKCDs were centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 20 min to remove unreacted precursors. The obtained clear supernatant solution of CKCDs was purified by dialysis membrane against DI water for 24 h applying a cellulose ester membrane (molecular weight cutoff: 2 kDa) to remove larger and unreacted reactants. The obtained solution was dried under hot-air oven and refrigerated for further functionalization reaction.

The solution containing CKCDs (10 mL, 20 mg mL−1), EDC (35 mg), and NHS (18 mg) were mixed in a beaker containing 20 mL phosphate-buffered saline (PBS; 10 mM, pH 5.5) and continuously stirred for 1 h in the dark conditions. Immediately, 4-VA (20 mg) was added to the above reaction mixture for facilitating EDC/NHS coupling for 10 h. Then, the obtained solution containing V-CKCDs were thoroughly centrifuged (8000 rpm, 20 min) and dialyzed (molecular weight cutoff: 2 kDa) for about 48 h to remove unconjugated 4-VA, CKCDs, and other reactants. Subsequently, the V-CKCDs were freeze dried at −50 °C for 24 h. The obtained dried powder was stored in the refrigerator for further experiments. The digital photographs of powdered CKCDs and V-CKCDs are shown in Fig. S1 . The steps for the synthesis of CKCDs and their functionalization via EDC/NHS coupling reaction to produce V-CKCDs are illustrated in Scheme 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of Hg2+ ions using V-CKCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The mixture containing Hg2+ (100 μL) and PBS (400 μL) were mixed in a cuvette consisting V-CKCDs (100 μL), and immediately incubated for 10 min. After the incubation, the photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained under the desired excitation wavelength (λex = 351 nm). The PL measurements were carried out for the detection of Hg2+ using the PL emission maximum of V-CKCDs around 449 nm.

The feasibility of the proposed V-CKCDs was investigated for determining Hg2+ using stream and tap water obtained from the Tancheon stream, Seongnam Si and the IT building, Gachon University, South Korea. These water samples were filtered through the membrane filter (0.22 μm) to remove bigger particles. Here, a 100-fold diluted real water samples were taken and spiked against different concentrations (0.05, 0.1, 0.2, and 0.5 µM) of Hg2+, and allowed to incubate with V-CKCDs for 30 min. Finally, the PL spectra were measured for obtaining recovery results. Further, diluted serum samples (100-fold) were spiked with different concentrations (0.5, 2.5, 3.5, and 4.0 µM) of Cys and incubated with V-CKCDs about 30 min. The corresponding fluorescence measurements were performed to obtain recovery and RSD values.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ and Cys in zebrafish larvae
ce:section.ce:para: Zebrafish larvae (CK112a) obtained from the Zebrafish Center for Disease Modeling (ZCDM), Chungnam National University, South Korea, were used as models for detecting the Hg2+ and Cys. The larvae were incubated with the V-CKCDs, V-CKCDs-Hg2+, V-CKCDs-Hg2+-Cys for about 5 h in the six-well plate at room temperature. After 5 h of incubation the sample solutions were removed and the zebrafish larvae were collected on to the glass slide and images were processed using confocal microscopy.

The morphology of the as-prepared CKCDs and V-CKCDs was studied under the visualization of TEM, SEM techniques and phase composition was analyzed through powder XRD technique. Fig. 1
A (yellow circles) and 1B represents the TEM and HRTEM images of CKCDs, which demonstrates its uniformity in dispersion with spherical-morphology. The inset in HRTEM image (left-lower side, Fig. 1 B) displays the lattice spacing around 0.215 nm, which signify the crystalline nature of CKCDs. The inset of HRTEM image showing the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern for CKCDs, which suggesting their crystallinity (right-upper side, Fig. 1 B). The particle size distribution curves for CKCDs revealed their average size about 4 nm as shown in Fig. 1 C. Further, the TEM (blue circles) and HRTEM images of V-CKCDs are exhibiting spherical shape and even distribution ( Fig. 1 D and Fig. 1 E). The inset of HRTEM image (right-upper side, Fig. 1 E) of V-CKCDs showing distinct lattice fringes present their crystallinity. Also, the SAED pattern for V-CKCDs is revealing their crystalline nature [(inset: left -upper side) Fig. 1 E]. The average size of the V-CKCDs was around 5 nm based on particle size distribution curves Fig. 1 F. The SEM analysis was performed for the CKCDs and V-CKCDs, which exhibited spherical morphology (
Fig. S2
). The SEM-EDX elemental mapping and spectra for the CKCDs reveal the elements carbon (C), oxygen (O), and nitrogen (N) as shown in (
Fig. S3 and S5
A). The V-CKCDs have shown the same elements along with potassium (K), chlorine (Cl), sodium (Na), and phosphorus (P) (
Fig. S4 and S5
B).

The FT-IR analysis of KMP, CKCDs, and V-CKCDs was investigated to study their functional groups. The FT-IR spectra for the KMP shows characteristic peaks at 1250, 1609, 1659 cm−1, which denote the stretching vibrations of (CO), (CC), and (CO) moieties. The other stretching vibrations of (CH) groups were observed at 2848 and 2916 cm−1. The other (CC) group’s stretching and in plane bending vibrations were observed at 1506 cm−1. The bending vibrations of (C-OH) groups were observed at 1174 and 1380 cm−1. The typical stretching vibrations of phenolic (–OH) groups were observed around 3077, 3122, 3201, 3308, and 3419 cm−1 ( Fig. 3
A). The FT-IR spectra for the CKCDs show peaks at 1254, 1308, 1370, and 1652 cm−1, which represent the stretching vibrations of (CO), (amide III, CN), (CN), and (CO) groups. The (CH) group’s stretching vibrations were observed at 2849 and 2916 cm−1. The bending vibrations of (NH) group was observed at 1558 cm−1. The (NH)/(OH) stretching vibrations were observed at 3279 and 3303 cm−1 ( Fig. 3 B). The FT-IR spectra of V-CKCDs demonstrate peaks at 1276, 1646 and 1700 cm−1, which indicate the stretching vibrations of (CO) and (CO) groups, respectively. The bending vibrations of (CH) and (NH) groups were observed at 1473 and 1558 cm−1, respectively. The typical deformation and stretching vibrations of (CH) moiety of vinyl groups was observed at 614 and 2971 cm−1, respectively. Other (CH) stretching vibrations were observed around 2849 and 2917 cm−1. The stretching vibrations of (NH)/(OH) groups were observed at 3368 cm−1 ( Fig. 3 C).

The chemical valence states of the CKCDs and V-CKCDs were studied through XPS analysis. The total survey spectra of CKCDs are revealing the elements including C 1 s, O 1 s, and N 1 s with binding energies of 285.17, 532.69, and 400.09 eV, respectively ( Fig. 4
A). The high-resolution spectra of C 1 s show deconvolutions with binding energies of 284.92, 286.69, and 288.15 eV, which assigned to the (CC/CC), (CN/CO), and (CO) groups ( Fig. 4 B). The high-resolution spectra of O 1 s show three peaks at 529.36, 531, and 532.81 eV, which correspond to the (CO), (COC)/(C-OH), and (CO) groups as shown in Fig. 4 C. The high-resolution spectra of N 1 s reveal two deconvoluted peaks at 399.73 and 401.66 eV indicating (C-NH) and (N-C3) groups ( Fig. 4 D). After the modification of CKCDs with 4-VA, the V-CKCDs have shown the total survey spectra for the elements C 1 s, O1s, N 1 s, K 2p, Cl 2p, Na 1 s, and P 2p with binding energies around 285.28, 532.22, 400.33, 293.49, 198.84, 1072.28, and 134.18 eV, respectively ( Fig. 5
A). The four deconvolutions of the high-resolution spectra of C1s include binding energies around 284.91, 286.44, 287.69, and 288.81 eV, which responsible for (CC/CC), (CN/CO), (CO), and (O-CO) groups, respectively ( Fig. 5 B). The high-resolution spectra of O 1 s have shown three peaks at 530.86, 532.63, and 534.7 eV, which ascribed to the (CO), (COC)/(C-OH), and (CO) groups, respectively ( Fig. 5 C). The two main peaks at 399.42 and 401.67 eV were observed in the high-resolution spectra of N 1 s, which assigned for (OC-NH/C-NH) and (N-C3) groups ( Fig. 5 D). The amide (OC-NH) bond formation in the N 1 s XPS spectra of V-CKCDs suggests the covalent reaction between free amine (–NH) groups of 4-VA and carboxyl (CO) groups of CKCDs via EDC/NHS chemistry. Due to the utilization of PBS buffer in the EDC/NHS coupling reaction, the high-resolution spectra for K 2p, Cl 2p, Na 1 s, and P 2p were also obtained. The two peaks at 292.26 and 295.01 eV were observed for the high-resolution spectra of K 2p, which attributed to the K 2p3/2 and K 2p1/2 chemical bondings ( Fig. 5 E). The high-resolution spectra of Cl 2p also have shown two deconvolutions at 197.31 and 199.1 eV, which belongs to the Cl 2p3/2 and Cl 2p1/2, respectively ( Fig. 5 F). Each of the high-resolution spectrum of Na 1 s and P 2p has shown a single deconvoluted peak at 1071.65 and 133.4 eV, respectively as shown in Fig. 5 G and Fig. 5 H.

After treating the V-CKCDs with Hg2+ ions, the total survey XPS spectra of V-CKCDs have showed the elements including C 1 s, O1s, N 1 s, K 2p, Cl 2p, Na 1 s, P 2p, and Hg 4f with binding energies of 285.06, 532.02, 400.27, 293.24, 198.43, 1071.93, 133.42, and 101.08 eV, respectively (
Fig. S9
A). The four peaks of the high-resolution spectra of C1s are including (CC/CC), (CN/CO), and (CO) at 284.78, 286.27, and 288.39 eV, respectively (
Fig. S9
B). The high-resolution spectra of O 1 s have shown four deconvolutions at 530.47, 531.78, 533.22, and 535.07 eV, which attributed to the (CO), (CO), (COC)/(C-OH), and (O-CO) groups, respectively (
Fig. S9
C). The two peaks were observed in the high-resolution spectra of N 1 s at 399.14 and 401.26 eV, which are responsible for (OC-NH/C-NH) and (N-C3) groups (
Fig. S9
D). The presence of amide (OC-NH) bond in the N 1 s XPS spectra of V-CKCDs has not affected by treating with Hg2+ ions. This suggests that V-CKCDs are adequately stable in the presence of ionic medium [42]. The two deconvolutions at 292.29 and 295.02 eV were observed in the high-resolution spectra of K 2p, which are responsible for K 2p3/2 and K 2p1/2 (
Fig. S9
E). The high-resolution spectra of Cl 2p also have shown two peaks at 197.55 and 198.92 eV, which assigned to the Cl 2p3/2 and Cl 2p1/2, as shown in Fig. S9
F. The high-resolution spectra of Na 1 s and P 2p have shown peaks around 1071.72 and 133.3 eV, respectively (
Fig. S9
G and S9H). The high-resolution spectra of V-CKCDs after treating with Hg2+ ions have shown four main peaks with binding energies of 100.43, 102.33, 104.4, and 105.61 eV. The two peaks at 100.43 and 104.4 eV indicating the metallic form of mercury (i.e., Hg0 4f7/2 and Hg 4f5/2) and the other two peaks at 102.33 and 105.61 eV representing their ionic forms (i.e., Hg2+ 4f7/2 and Hg 4f5/2), respectively (
Fig. S9
I). These results are suggesting that Hg2+ has reduced to Hg0 by V-CKCDs, which enabled their high sensitivity [43].

The UV–vis absorption spectra for the KMP, CKCDs, and V-CKCDs were studied to investigate their absorption phenomena ( Fig. 6
A). The KMP has shown two bands at 270 and 374 nm, which represent the characteristic ring-A portion (band II, benzoyl) and ring-B portions (band I, cinnamoyl), respectively (green band). After the formation of the CKCDs, the absorption band was newly formed at 210 nm, which indicated the π-π* transition. The other absorption bands were slightly blue-shifted to 268 and 368 nm, which are attributed to the n-π* transitions owing to the formation of (CN) bonds. However, after the functionalization of CKCDs with 4-VA, the absorption bands were formed around 261 and 368 nm. The insets of Fig. 6 A shows bright orange-colored and strong blue-colored emissions of CKCDs and V-CKCDs, respectively under the UV-light (365 nm). The PL properties of CKCDs were studied using PL measurements, which displayed maximum PL excitation and emission wavelengths of 467 and 550 nm, respectively in their PL excitation (black curve) and emission spectra (
Fig. S10
). Unfortunately, a decreased PL emission property was observed in the fluorescence spectra of CKCDs, which results from the less solubility of KMP in aqueous solutions. However, V-CKCDs has showed a distinguished fluorescence property in their PL excitation (black curve) and emission (red curve) spectra, which display λmax at 351 and 449 nm, respectively ( Fig. 6 B). The high fluorescence response of these V-CKCDs compared to that of CKCDs may have attributed to their increased aromatic carbon structure and amine (–NH2) content [44,45]. The V-CKCDs have exhibited excitation-dependent emission property under the altered excitation wavelengths from 300 to 500 nm as shown in Fig. 6 C. This property was ascribed to the availability of distinguished surface states over the V-CKCDs and also these results are correlating with the other functionalized CDs [46]. The quantum yield values for the CKCDs and V-CKCDs was found to be 13.8 % and 17.6 %, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity studies using V-CKCDs for Hg2+ and Cys detection
ce:section.ce:para:
The V-CKCDs have shown the significant sensitivity under the different concentrations of Hg2+ (0–0.5 μM) as shown in Fig. 7
A. An adequate linearity was found between (F0-F/ F0) against various concentrations of Hg2+ (0–0.2 μM) with a good R2 value around 0.997 ( Fig. 7 B). According to the 3σ/K, the limit of detection (LOD) was found to be 10.6 nM for the detection of Hg2+. The obtained high sensitivity is corroborating with the maximum allowed limit (̃30 nM) for Hg2+ in drinking water as per the world health organization (WHO) guidelines [47]. Table 1 shows the comparison for the proposed sensor with formerly reported analytical probes [31,33,47–53]. Again, the fluorescence enhancement was observed in the case of V-CKCDs-Hg2+ complex, after adding different concentrations of Cys ranging from 0 to 4 μM ( Fig. 7 C). A good linear relationship was found between (F-F0/ F0) and Cys in the concentration range of 0–3.5 μM with R2 value of 0.999 and the calculated LOD was around 42.48 nM ( Fig. 7 D). The achieved high sensitivity is well-consistent with the previously reported fluorescent sensors as summarized in Table 2
[31,48,54–60]. The sensitivity of the proposed fluorometric biosensor was studied using the lifetime decay analysis (
Fig. S11
). The curve fitting for the decay curves was achieved based on the following double exponential equation:
D
( t )
=
∑
i = 1

τ i
)

The PL decay, lifetime, and pre-exponential factors are demonstrated by D, τi, and ai, respectively. The V-CKCDs alone showed the lifetimes of τ1 around 8.51 ns and τ2 around 0.018 ns. After the treatment of Hg2+ (0.5 μM) to the V-CKCDs, the lifetime values were rapidly declined to τ1 of 7.95 ns and τ2 0.016 ns. Further, when Cys (4 μM) was added to the complex containing V-CKCDs-Hg2+, the lifetime values were dramatically increased with τ1 of 9.12 ns and τ2 0.022 ns. We believe that this might be responsible for the dynamic electron transfer between V-CKCDs and Hg2+, which leads to the fluorescence quenching in the V-CKCDs. Again, the possible electron transfer from the complex i.e., V-CKCDs-Hg2+ to the Cys, which recover the fluorescence in V-CKCDs. The plausible turn-off–on mechanism for the developed V-CKCDs is illustrated in Fig. 8 .

The selectivity of the V-CKCDs was examined by comparing the PL intensity of V-CKCDs alone and Hg2+ with other metal ions such as Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Fe3+, Fe2+, and Ni2+ in the presence of V-CKCDs. The concentrations other interferants (5 μM) were taken 10-fold higher as compared to that of Hg2+ (0.5 μM). The significant difference was found in the PL quenching of V-CKCDs, when treated with Hg2+ (Ca. 50 %), meanwhile very negligible PL quenching was observed in the V-CKCDs, when treated with other interferants ( Fig. 9
A). Moreover, the selectivity of V-CKCDs was checked by adding Hg2+ (0.5 μM) to the other interferants (5 μM) using PL measurements ( Fig. 9 B). Noteworthy, the PL quenching of V-CKCDs was observed against Hg2+ even in the presence of other interferants. We believed this response might have attributed to the convenient chemical interactions between Hg2+ and excessive (–OH) and (-CO) moieties of the V-CKCDs. Noteworthy, the zeta potential measurements are also suggesting that the V-CKCDs were well interacted with Hg2+, which enabled the shift of surface charge from a negative value (-11.73 mV) to a positive value (0.35 mV). Likewise, the magnificent quenching capabilities of Hg2+ towards the fluorescence of V-CKCDs in contrast to the other metal ions are beneficial for their high selectivity [61,62]. Again, an effective heteroatom doping in the V-CKCDs facilitating the greater quenching efficiency against Hg2+ than other interferants. These results are strongly corroborating with the previously reported nitrogen (N)-doped CDs against Hg2+ [63,64]. These results are recommending the reasonable selectivity of V-CKCDs towards the Hg2+ sensing. Further, the selectivity of the V-CKCDs-Hg2+ complex was studied using twenty essential aminoacids including Ala, Arg, Asn, Asp, Gln, Glu, His, Ile, Leu, Lys, Met, Phe, Pro, Ser, Thr, Trp, Tyr, and Val against Cys ( Fig. 9 C). The concentrations of Cys (4 μM) and 5-fold concentrations of above-mentioned aminoacids (20 μM) were used to measure their PL emission spectra. The Cys has shown the higher selectivity with maximum fluorescence enhancement in the V-CKCDs-Hg2+ complex as compared to that of the complex treated with other interferants. The negligible fluorescence was delivered by the complex in the presence of interferants, which suggests the V-CKCDs are reliable and highly selective towards the detection of Cys in contrast to the contemporary interferants. The achieved sensitivity and selectivity for the V-CKCDs against Hg2+ may have result from the convenient electrostatic interactions between Hg2+ cations and negatively charged anions (–OH groups) of the V-CKCDs. Further, the obtained reasonable sensitivity of V-CKCDs against Cys detection was attributed to the favorable interactions between Hg2+ present in the complex of V-CKCDs- Hg2+ and the thiol (-SH) moieties available from the Cys. Again, the stability of the V-CKCDs was studied under different pH values ranging from 3 to 12, which showed the higher PL intensity around pH 5.5 (
Fig. S12
A). However, the PL intensity of V-CKCDs was slightly increased and decreased in acidic and alkaline pH conditions due to their protonation and deprotonation effects [65]. The V-CKCDs has shown the good stability without losing their PL intensity over 180 days at the intervals of 15 days (
Fig. S12
B). The long-term stability delivered by the V-CKCDs is highly beneficial for studying biological applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practicality of V-CKCDs in real sample analysis

ce:section.ce:para: We used zebrafish larvae as the in vivo models to detect V-CKCDs, V-CKCDs, V-CKCDs-Hg2+, and V-CKCDs-Hg2+-Cys. Fig. 10 demonstrates the imaging of zebrafish larvae using confocal microscopy with the excitation range 405 nm. Fig. 10 A shows the blue fluorescence of V-CKCDs alone (control) without treating with Hg2+ in the zebrafish larvae and after treating V-CKCDs with Hg2+ (0.5 µM), the diminished fluorescence was seen in the larvae as shown in Fig. 10 B. Furthermore, when the Cys (4.0 µM) was treated with V-CKCDs-Hg2+ complex, the fluorescence recovery was observed in the zebrafish larvae ( Fig. 10 C). This suggests the potential sensitivity of the proposed V-CKCDs against Hg2+ and Cys with a well-distribution in the zebrafish larvae.

In this study, we developed the fluorometric biosensor using citric acid and polyphenolic kaempferol in the DMF to synthesize CKCDs and further using EDC/NHS coupling the vinyl-functionalized CDs (i.e., V-CKCDs) were prepared. These V-CKCDs can be utilized as the “turn-off–on” fluorescent sensor for detecting Hg2+ and Cys. The high sensitivity of V-CKCDs against Hg2+ and Cys showed detection limits around 10.6 and 42.48 nM, respectively. The practical applications of the proposed sensor were investigated in stream and tap water samples and in human serum for determining Hg2+ and Cys. The in vivo study was demonstrated in zebrafish larvae using V-CKCDs for Hg2+ and Cys sensing. The high thermal and long-term stabilities were found in the V-CKCDs. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of vinyl-functionalization to the carbon dots for developing turn-off–on sensor against Hg2+ and Cys. This work contributes a prospective scope in understanding the construction of carbon dot-based biosensors for the detection of chemical hazards and biomolecules.
